macro_segments.title: Introduction and the Prohibition Against Sanctifying the Se'ir HaMishtale'ach with a Blemish

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context of the discussion on blemished animals
The question of why the se'ir hamishtalach must be unblemished given the requirement that it be fit for either outcome of the lottery
Rav Yosef's citation of Chananya HaMitzri and the debate over whether a replacement animal requires a new lottery
Rava's scenario involving a blemished animal after the lottery, and the principle that you do not need a new lottery when replacing a blemished designated animal
The principle that transferring sanctity to a replacement animal avoids the prohibition of temurah and does not require a new lottery

macro_segments.title: Expansion of the Prohibition: Multiple Stages of the Service and Rabbi Yose bar Rabbi Yehuda's View on Catching the Blood

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yose bar Rabbi Yehuda's position that catching the blood of a blemished animal is also prohibited
Attempt to derive catching the blood from the verse about mutilated animals, and the objection that this verse appears merely rhetorical
Alternative source from the verse prohibiting acceptance from non-Jews as the basis for the prohibition on catching blood
Explanation of why non-Jews may have different standards for blemished animals on their own altars versus in the Beit HaMikdash
Second version of the debate between Rabbi Yose bar Rabbi Yehuda and the Rabbis regarding different verses and their interpretations
The Rabbis' position that the verse about non-Jews teaches that blemished animals cannot be brought even on private altars

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Challenge: When Does the Prohibition Apply to Animals with Blemishes?

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's question whether the prohibition applies only when an animal became blemished after being unblemished, not when it was born blemished
Rashi's psychological explanation that an animal born blemished is treated differently because it was wasted potential
Tosafot's interpretation focusing on the level of sanctity: kedushat mizbe'ach only applies if the animal started unblemished
Rabbi Chiya bar Rav Yosef's proof that the prohibition includes animals with birth defects like sarua and kalut
Reish Lakish's limitation to temurah: perhaps the prohibition only applies when doing temurah, where full sanctity can be conferred even on a blemished animal
Rabbi Yochanan's response citing the vote of Rabbi Yannai that five transgressions apply even to animals with birth defects
The resolution that although sanctity is not full kedushat mizbe'ach when starting with a blemish, the act is still offensive and prohibited

macro_segments.title: Extension to Non-Sacrificial Uses: Sanctifying Blemished Animals for Nesachim and Treasury

micro_segments.title: Rava's extension that even sanctifying a blemished animal for its proceeds to be used for wine offerings (nesachim) is prohibited
Derivation from the verse 'nedavta ta'aseh oto u'lneder lo yiratzeh' teaching that blemished animals go to temple treasury and unblemished to sacrifice
The debate between Tanna Kamma and Rabbi about whether the prohibition applies to using proceeds for non-sacrificial purposes
The principle that one cannot sanctify an unblemished animal for temple treasury, as this violates the proper categorization
Derivation of a positive commandment and negative prohibition against sanctifying unblemished animals for treasury using the word 'oto' and the phrase 'vayidaber Hashem el Moshe le'amor'

macro_segments.title: Multiple Transgressions from a Single Act of Bringing a Blemished Animal

micro_segments.title: The question of whether burning part and burning all of a blemished animal's parts constitute one or two separate transgressions
Abaye's principle that generic prohibitions (lav shebechlalot) do not result in multiple sets of lashes
Textual difficulties and attempted resolutions regarding whether five or four transgressions apply to blemished animals

macro_segments.title: Temurah by Kohanim and the First Mishna of the Next Section

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the mishna that kohanim can perform temurah on their animals, but not on chatat, asham, or bechor that belong to a Yisrael
The principle that a kohen cannot perform temurah on a chatat or asham even though he will eat the meat, because they remain the owner's sacrifice
The surprising principle that a kohen cannot perform temurah on a bechor, even though it is given to him as a complete gift, because the original sanctity occurred in the owner's house
Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri's objection to the analogy between bechor and chatat/asham, and Rabbi Akiva's response using the verse about temurah occurring where sanctity originated

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yose bar Rabbi Yehuda (catching blood)
